exemplar.title:
What is the effect of different starting stances of crossover stance, frontfoot forward, and backfoot on the base on the sprint time (s) from base to base in slow-pitch softball in high school female students aged 17-18 year olds
who know how to play softball?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

detailedExemplarData.title:
What is the effect of different starting stances of crossover stance, frontfoot forward, and backfoot on the base on the sprint time (s) from base to base in slow-pitch softball in high school female students aged 17-18 year olds
who know how to play softball?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

strengths: Choice of topic clearly rooted in personal experience
Sustained evidence of independent design decisions (randomisation, pilot testing)
Justification directly linked to studentâs own sporting context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands show clear personal engagement, significant independent thinking and direct personal significance. The student designs bespoke procedures, statistical analyses and links the investigation to their own practiceâfully meeting the topâband descriptors for a score of 2.

strengths: Focused and relevant research question identifying specific variables
Thorough background citing multiple biomechanical sources
Highly appropriate methodology with randomisation, controlled variables, pilot testing
Comprehensive safety and ethical measures with detailed risk table
weaknesses: Some background material is tangential (e.g., volleyball studies)
Minor detail missing on random number generator implementation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite minor tangential elements and a slightly verbose question wording, the investigation consistently demonstrates a fully focused, relevant research question, entirely appropriate background, a robust methodology addressing key factors and full safety/ethical awareness. These features align with the 5â6 band across all strands, justifying the maximum score of 6.

strengths: Complete raw data set for all participants and conditions
Accurate calculation of means, SDs, CVs, ANOVA and post hoc tests
Correct use of boxâandâwhisker plots to identify outliers
Clear example calculation for coefficient of variation
weaknesses: No full propagation of measurement uncertainty into inferential statistics
Minor formula misprints and absence of uncertainty bars on graphs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates accurate and appropriate data processing, correct use of inferential statistics and effective graphical interpretation. Some limitations remain in full uncertainty treatment and minor formula presentation, placing performance at the high end of the 3â6 band but just short of perfection, meriting a score of 5.

strengths: Conclusions tied directly to statistical outcomes
Clear discussion of methodological limitations and practical issues
weaknesses: Conclusion and context discussion could be deeper (effect sizes missing)
Some inconsistencies in evaluation table formatting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation offers a detailed, relevant conclusion supported by data, integrates with scientific context, demonstrates clear methodological insight and provides realistic, relevant improvements. Minor gaps in effectâsize discussion and formatting prevent the absolute top band, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

strengths: Clear, logical presentation with minimal errors
Well-structured report using appropriate headings, tables and figures
weaknesses: Occasional formatting glitches and typographical errors
Some redundancy in appendices and citation inconsistencies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The communication is consistently clear, well organized and uses appropriate terminology with minimal errors, fully meeting the descriptors for a topâband score of 3â4. Minor presentation and consistency issues justify the maximum mark of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Uncertainty is acknowledged by quoting gate precision (Â±0.01 s) and using SD/CV, but no combined uncertainty or error bars for measurement precision are carried through to the inferential statistics.  This demonstrates âsome considerationâ rather than full treatment, matching a score of 3.

The student references several peer-reviewed studies and contrasts their findings with current results, showing some integration into the scientific context.  Discussion is correct but not deeply justified (e.g., fails to quantify effect sizes), so a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

The investigation follows a logical sequence (introduction, method, results, evaluation) and uses sub-headings and tables effectively.  Occasional redundancy in appendices prevents full conciseness, but overall organisation meets the criteria for a 3 in the 3â4 band.

The student proposes specific, feasible improvements (marking bases, sound-cue starts, scheduling to minimise wet turf) and outlines how each would address identified weaknesses.  The suggestions are detailed and relevant, but not comprehensive enough for top-band 5-6, so a 4 is awarded.

The research question is relevant and largely focused (effect of three specific starting stances on sprint time in a defined population).  Although wording is somewhat verbose, the variables are identifiable and limited, so the focus is maintained throughout the report; this aligns with the ârelevant but not fully focusedâ descriptor, meriting the upper end (4) of the 3â4 band.

The evaluation table identifies methodological and practical weaknesses (foot placement, prior fatigue, field moisture) and discusses their significance, indicating good methodological insight.  While some points are brief, the overall understanding is clear, justifying the upper value (4) in the 3â4 band.

Methodology addresses sampling, randomisation, warm-up, controlled variables and pilot testing, showing thoughtful planning.  However, key limitations remain (small sample, inconsistently controlled footwear, wet turf for some trials).  Because several significant factors are only partially managed, the work fits âmainly appropriate with limitationsâ and earns 3 within the 3â4 band.

The background section cites multiple biomechanical and sport-specific sources, explains each stance, and links external findings to the current study.  Some material (e.g., volleyball studies, generic warm-up rationales) is tangential, so coverage is âmainly appropriate and relevantâ rather than exhaustive.  Hence a solid mid-band score (4) is appropriate.

Most content is directly relevant to answering the research question; however, lengthy quotations and duplicated references slightly obscure key findings.  The report remains sufficiently focused for readers to follow the argument, warranting a 3.

Interpretation identifies which pairwise comparisons are significant and links findings to biomechanics literature.  Nonetheless, the conclusion overstates reliability given sample limitations and mis-states CV values.  Interpretation is âbroadly valid but incompleteâ, meriting 3.

Figures, tables and headings make the report generally easy to follow, though some formatting glitches and duplicated material occur.  Minor typographical errors do not impede understanding, fitting the descriptor of âclear presentation with minimal errorsâ; a score of 3 is appropriate.

The studentâs choice of topic clearly stems from personal playing experience and curiosity about coaching advice, and this personal perspective is woven throughout the report (e.g., pilot testing the gates after noticing a failure in Trial 1).  The design shows initiative in randomising stance order, pilot-testing apparatus and compiling qualitative observationsâactions that go beyond teacher-led procedures and indicate independent thinking.  These features align well with the descriptor for âclear evidence of personal engagement with significant independent thinkingâ.

Means, SDs, coefficients of variation, ANOVA and Bonferroni-corrected t-tests are calculated correctly, and graphs include error bars.  Minor formula misprints and absence of uncertainty propagation slightly reduce accuracy, yet overall processing is appropriate and mostly accurate, warranting 4 in the 3â4 band.

The justification links directly to the studentâs own playing experience and to the practical significance for high-school coaches and athletes, establishing obvious personal relevance.  The introduction explicitly states why resolving the stance debate matters to the student and to the sport, demonstrating curiosity rather than simply fulfilling an assignment requirement.

Raw data include three trials for 10 participants across three conditions (â90 points) and are tabulated in full; this is relevant but modest in quantity and one participant shows an anomalous value (2.78 s) without replication.  Thus the strand meets ârelevant but incompleteâ expectations, deserving a 3.

The investigation evidences personal input at every stage: construction of the warm-up, rest protocol, risk table, randomisation procedure, and bespoke qualitative questionnaire.  The student also designs and executes the statistical analyses (ANOVA, Bonferroni-adjusted t tests) independently.  Such sustained initiative throughout planning, implementation and presentation fulfils the highest band requirements.

Comprehensive safety and ethical measures are evident: PAR-Q screening, informed consent, hydration and heat protocols, postponed trials if conditions unsafe, and a detailed risk table with mitigation strategies.  These actions show full awareness, though not every risk (e.g., COVID protocols) is mentioned, so a 5 (rather than 6) in the top band is justified.

Subject-specific terms (e.g., coefficient of variation, Bonferroni correction) are used correctly and units are mostly consistent.  Citation style is uneven and a few formulae are mistyped, so the work meets âappropriate use with minimal errorsâ rather than exemplary accuracy, justifying 3.

The conclusion restates statistical outcomes and addresses hypotheses, linking directly to processed data; however, inconsistencies (e.g., calling coefficients âhighâ then using them for reliability claims) weaken full support.  This fits the middle band with a score of 3.

annotations.comment: The research question is overly verbose and combines multiple variables and conditions. Narrow the focus by rephrasing it more concisely, specifying only the essential independent and dependent variables.
Some background material (e.g., volleyball studies) is tangential to slow-pitch softball. Focus on directly relevant biomechanical research and trim extraneous examples to strengthen relevance.
The final sentence of the introduction cuts off mid-thought. Ensure each paragraph is complete and transitions clearly into the purpose of the investigation.
Hypotheses H1 and H2 overlap conceptually. Clarify the null and alternative hypotheses separately and align them precisely with the research question.
The PAR-Q description references Appendix f before it is introduced. Reorder or cross-reference appendices consistently to avoid confusion.
Randomisation is mentioned but lacks detail on how the generator was implemented and verified. Provide specifics on the random number algorithm and its validation.
The pilot test paragraph describes gate errors but omits quantitative pilot data (e.g., timing differences). Include pilot data summaries to justify apparatus choice.
The equipment table omits measurement uncertainty for many items (e.g., trundle wheel). Provide error tolerances or state 'Â±' values for each apparatus.
Table 2 header is empty and misaligned. Remove placeholder or fill in controlled variables table properly to communicate content clearly.
The confounding variables table label 'muscles used in sprinting' is mispositioned above recovery time. Correct table titles to match content.
The safety paragraph is succinct and demonstrates comprehensive awareness of ethical and safety procedures including PAR-Q, consent, and withdrawal rights.
Risk table omits mitigation for displaced bases over time. Suggest adding procedures to re-measure and re-align bases after each block of trials.
Participant 4âs 2.78 s trial is a clear outlier in the raw data. Discuss whether to repeat or exclude anomalous values before analysis.
The narrative mentions raw data in Appendix g/h but does not clearly reference how it informed processing. Link data collection to subsequent steps explicitly.
Raw data tables show individual trials but lack an initial summary of means/SDs. Include participant-level summaries here for immediate reference.
The SD formula is positioned after the mean equation, but the actual formula block is missing. Ensure each statistical formula is correctly displayed.
Description of data processing refers to spreadsheet functions inaccurately (e.g., âââSTDEVâ). Clarify the correct functions and formulas used.
Coefficient of variation equation is correctly presented and justifies its use in comparing variability across stances.
The example calculation of 1.94 % CoV is clear and correctly applies the formula to participant 1âs data.
Processed data table omits combined uncertainty propagation. Consider including error bars or confidence intervals to reflect measurement precision.
Box-and-whisker plot effectively indicates absence of outliers. Good choice for preliminary distribution checks.
Processed summary table lacks number of participants (n) entry. Add an explicit ân=10â label to clarify sample size per stance.
Effect sizes or confidence intervals are not reported alongside p-values. Include effect size metrics to contextualize statistical significance.
Conclusion misinterprets H2. It states H2 is rejected based on two non-significant comparisons but does not fully address H2 scope. Clarify logic.
Literature discussion repeats the same source twice (Krause 2016). Consolidate citations and avoid redundancy.
Generalizability is noted as limited but lacks concrete suggestions for future sample expansion (e.g., include collegiate players). Add specific next-phase sampling ideas.
Evaluation table lists strengths but evaluation categories are inconsistent (e.g., some rows have no âWeaknessâ label). Standardize format for clarity.
Last suggestion entry is incomplete in improvement table. Finish sentence and clarify planned sampling criteria for participant skill levels.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: Justification shows little personal significance or curiosity
Little evidence of personal input in design, implementation or presentation
Limited evidence of personal engagement with little independent thinking
Clear evidence of personal input and initiative throughout the investigation
Clear evidence of personal engagement with significant independent thinking
Justification demonstrates clear personal significance and curiosity

rubricCriterionStrands.name: Evidence of personal engagement and independent thinking

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: Superficial or limited relevance background information
Research question stated but not focused
Methodology appropriate to very limited extent
Mainly appropriate and relevant background information
Relevant but not fully focused research question
Entirely appropriate and relevant background information
Highly appropriate methodology addressing all significant factors
Relevant and fully focused research question
Sufficient relevant raw data supporting detailed conclusion
Basic outline of practical issues only
Some relevant comparison to scientific context
Detailed, relevant and fully supported conclusion
Clear understanding of methodological issues demonstrated
Correct and justified comparison to scientific context
Many errors in terminology and conventions
Appropriate use of terminology with minimal errors
Well structured with all necessary information
